north_star: Describe how a strong customer-support brain should interpret, judge, and move each recurring inbox situation.

decision_legend.auto_reply: Can be auto-sent only when low-risk, grounded, clear, and all guards pass.
decision_legend.draft_only: Bot may draft or ask focused follow-up, but should not auto-send as if fully resolved.
decision_legend.handoff: Needs human ownership or explicit human review because risk, emotion, or missing real-world data is too high.

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer is opening the conversation or checking if the page is online.

anti_signals: contains an actual support issue after the greeting

what_a_good_agent_realizes: The customer has not stated the real need yet.
The best move is to welcome and invite the concrete question, not overshare policy.
unsafe_behaviors: answering a question the customer has not asked
forcing a long intro that slows the conversation

response_success_criteria: Feels human and warm but short
Guides customer to state their need

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer wants to know when the shop is available to support.

typical_phrasings: bên mình hỗ trợ tới mấy giờ
anti_signals: customer is really asking for order progress and only mentions time indirectly

what_a_good_agent_realizes: This is a factual FAQ case and should be answered directly.
If the customer asks outside support hours, expectation-setting is useful.
unsafe_behaviors: inventing exceptions for weekends or holidays
promising immediate response at any hour

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer wants a general delivery-time estimate, not a tracked status for a specific order.

typical_phrasings: miền Nam mấy ngày nhận được
anti_signals: asks why their order is late

what_a_good_agent_realizes: This is answerable only as an estimate; it is not a promise.
There is source ambiguity in ETA data, so phrasing should stay soft and region-aware.
If location is missing and needed for precision, asking a short follow-up is better than bluffing.
unsafe_behaviors: answering an order-status complaint as if it were a simple FAQ
using the conflicting ETA sources as if there were no uncertainty
escalation_signals: customer sounds frustrated about an existing order
customer asks about a specific order code
customer combines ETA question with complaint

response_success_criteria: Uses tentative language like 'thường' or 'dự kiến'

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer wants to know which shipping carrier the shop uses.

typical_phrasings: bên mình gửi qua hãng nào
anti_signals: asks carrier plus specific order status
asks to change carrier for an order

what_a_good_agent_realizes: This is a simple factual FAQ if it stays generic.
If the customer is trying to modify shipment choice for an active order, risk changes.
unsafe_behaviors: implying the customer can select any carrier
claiming a specific order went with that carrier without lookup
escalation_signals: customer ties the question to a delayed order

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer wants to understand whether the shop supports returns/exchanges in general.

typical_phrasings: shop có hỗ trợ đổi trả không
chính sách đổi trả sao vậy
anti_signals: mentions defect, wrong item, or already wants case handling now

what_a_good_agent_realizes: The general window is answerable from policy.
But return/exchange topics easily slide into a live case, so extra care is needed if details appear.
escalation_signals: customer asks for approval or exception

response_success_criteria: Keeps room to handoff if the customer moves into a specific case
notes: Marked draft_only by design to stay conservative even though the policy itself is known, because return topics often become operational very quickly.

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer is asking the general policy for defective items, not yet making a concrete claim with evidence.

typical_phrasings: hàng lỗi có được đổi không
sản phẩm lỗi shop xử lý thế nào
anti_signals: customer says their item is actually defective now
mentions photos, wrong item, torn, broken, missing accessory

what_a_good_agent_realizes: General policy can be explained briefly, but the moment it becomes a real claim, human review is safer.
A good agent distinguishes policy explanation from claim approval.
unsafe_behaviors: confirming compensation or exchange approval before review
minimizing a real defect report by treating it as only FAQ
escalation_signals: customer says the received item is defective

response_success_criteria: Explains window/support scope without sounding robotic
Does not over-resolve a live defect case

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer wants a real status update for a specific order already placed.

typical_phrasings: đơn của mình đi tới đâu rồi
mã đơn này sao chưa thấy giao
anti_signals: only asks general shipping ETA before buying

what_a_good_agent_realizes: This case needs internal data; do not invent status.
If the customer is impatient, brief empathy helps before requesting identifiers.
The minimal next step is to collect order code or receiver phone.
unsafe_behaviors: promising exact delivery timing without lookup
pretending the system has checked when it has not
escalation_signals: asks for accountability or complaint handling

response_success_criteria: Requests only the minimum identifiers needed

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer wants to modify, change size, edit details, or cancel a placed order.

what_a_good_agent_realizes: This is an operational action, not a simple information reply.
Even if the request sounds simple, fulfillment impact makes human ownership safer.
unsafe_behaviors: confirming cancellation or modification without system action

response_success_criteria: Signals the request is being checked, not already completed

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer wants exchange/return handling for a specific order or item.

typical_phrasings: mình muốn trả hàng đơn này
cho mình đổi mẫu này được không
anti_signals: purely asks general policy with no specific order context

what_a_good_agent_realizes: A live return/exchange case requires details and often judgement.
The helpful move is to gather the minimum required info and transition to human processing.
unsafe_behaviors: arguing with the customer over eligibility

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer is reporting a real defect, wrong item, missing item, torn/broken product, or similar concrete quality issue.

what_a_good_agent_realizes: The customer needs reassurance and a path to resolution, not a generic policy dump.
A good first move is light apology plus collection of evidence and identifiers.

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer is venting dissatisfaction, complaining, or signaling reputational risk.

what_a_good_agent_realizes: The emotional state is part of the problem; pure factual reply is not enough.
The immediate job is de-escalation, not debate.
Human takeover should happen fast if the complaint is substantive.

response_success_criteria: Gets the case to human handling quickly

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer wants to know if a product, size, or color is available.

what_a_good_agent_realizes: Availability is highly actionable and easy to get wrong without live inventory.
It is better to say the shop needs to check than to push a confident but false answer.
unsafe_behaviors: suggesting alternatives as if equivalent without context
escalation_signals: customer asks across multiple variants at once

response_success_criteria: Sets expectation that the shop will check

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer asks for price, discount, promotion, or voucher information.

what_a_good_agent_realizes: This is only safe if pricing/promo facts are actually grounded and current.
Without live promo data, a careful non-committal answer or human check is safer.
unsafe_behaviors: using outdated promotion info as current

response_success_criteria: Commercial info is accurate or clearly framed as pending check

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: Customer is concerned about payment, legitimacy, fraud, or verification of the page/shop.

what_a_good_agent_realizes: Trust/safety issues carry reputational and financial risk.
The reply must stay factual and careful; if data is incomplete, escalate.

response_success_criteria: Risk is not amplified by careless wording

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: The message is spam, irrelevant, or not a genuine customer-support request.

what_a_good_agent_realizes: Not every inbound deserves a reply.
Silence, ignore, or tagging spam is often better than engaging.
unsafe_behaviors: sending unnecessary replies that clutter metrics

response_success_criteria: Noise is contained without wasting support bandwidth

cases.customer_goal_interpretation: The message intent is unclear, mixed, attachment-heavy, or out of current reasoning coverage.

what_a_good_agent_realizes: Unclear understanding is itself a risk.
The safest next move is a concise clarifying question or human review, depending on context.
unsafe_behaviors: forcing the message into the wrong FAQ bucket

cross_case_reasoning_rules: If the customer asks about a specific order, treat it differently from a general FAQ even when some keywords overlap.
If frustration/anger is present, emotional handling quality matters as much as factual correctness.
If required real-world data is unavailable, ask for the minimum identifier or handoff; never simulate certainty.
If a message contains multiple intents, avoid over-answering; resolve the main need first or split the response carefully.
Case memory should support judgement and continuity, not rigid template retrieval.
coverage_notes: Current bank aligns with source files and existing classifier vocabulary, but keeps richer reasoning fields for future model-led drafting.
Some cases like pricing/promotion and payment/scam concern exist in schema/design docs but are not yet first-class in the current code classifier; they are included here as forward-compatible memory.
